perturbation.original_text: The DISCLOSEE may not make confidential information available within its enterprise or organisation other than to staff who necessarily re quire it to perform their duties. Such persons must demonstrably be bound beforehand to keep the inform ation confidential to the same strict extent as agreed herein, including after their contractual re lationships are terminated insofar as the law allow s. The DISCLOSEE will be liable for any and all losses the DISCLOSER suffers.
perturbation.changed_text: The DISCLOSEE may make confidential information available within its enterprise or organization to staff as reasonably determined to require it to perform their duties. Such persons should be encouraged to keep the information confidential. The DISCLOSEE will be liable for losses the DISCLOSER suffers due to gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. 'As reasonably determined' is subjective, and 'should be encouraged' is less binding than 'must demonstrably be bound.' Limiting liability to 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' significantly raises the bar for the DISCLOSER to claim damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian Civil Code § 1295 establishes general liability for damages caused by fault. By limiting the DISCLOSEE's liability to gross negligence or willful misconduct, the modified text contradicts the broader standard of liability under Austrian law, which includes ordinary negligence.

Nr. 96/1868 zuletzt geändert durch BGBl. I Nr. 71/2022 Typ BG §/Artikel/Anlage § 24a Inkrafttretensdatum 01.07.2023 Außerkrafttretensdatum Abkürzung RAO Index 27/01 Rechtsanwälte Text § 24a. Paragraph 24 a, (1) Absatz eins Die Geschäftsordnung der Rechtsanwaltskammer kann vorsehen, dass die Wahlen nach § 24 Abs. 1 auch im Weg der Übermittlung eines verschlossenen Kuverts an die Rechtsanwaltskammer erfolgen können (Briefwahl). Beabsichtigt ein Kammermitglied, sein Wahlrecht im Weg der Briefwahl auszuüben, so hat es davon die Rechtsanwaltskammer bis spätestens drei Wochen vor dem Tag der Plenarversammlung zu informieren. Diese hat an das Kammermitglied spätestens zehn Tage vor dem Wahltag den oder die Stimmzettel, ein verschließbares Wahlkuvert sowie ein mit der Anschrift der Rechtsanwaltskammer als Empfänger und dem Namen des betreffenden Kammermitglieds als Absender bedrucktes und verschließbares Rückkuvert zu übermitteln. Die Wahlkuverts für Rechtsanwälte und Rechtsanwaltsanwärter müssen verschiedenfarbig sein. Auf der Rückseite des Rückkuverts ist folgender Satz anzubringen: „Mit meiner Unterschrift erkläre ich eidesstattlich, dass ich den/die einliegenden Stimmzettel persönlich, unbeobachtet und unbeeinflusst ausgefüllt habe.“ Die Geschäftsordnung der Rechtsanwaltskammer kann vorsehen, dass die Wahlen nach Paragraph 24, Absatz eins, auch im Weg der Übermittlung eines verschlossenen Kuverts an die Rechtsanwaltskammer erfolgen können (Briefwahl). perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Austrian Civil Code ) Civil code of Austria This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( March 2024 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) ABGB from 1811, Museum of Military History, Vienna The General civil code ( German : A llgemeines b ürgerliches G esetz b uch or ABGB ) is the Civil Code of Austria , which after about 40 years of preparatory work was published on 1 June 1811 and came into force on 1 January 1812. [ 1 ] Karl Anton Freiherr von Martini and Franz von Zeiller were the leading drafters at the earlier and later stages of the draft. Comparable to the Napoleonic code , it was based on the ideals of freedom and equality before the law. It was divided into three major segments, following the Roman law segregation methods. It was modernized during the First World War . ABGB continues to be the basic civil code of Austria to this day and it is also still the basic civil code of Liechtenstein . The ABGB was widely received. Besides Austria, its influence persists in other successor states of Austria-Hungary . In the Czech part of Czechoslovakia (the Slovak part used Hungarian customary law) it was in effect until 1951, although it had been novelized multiple times, until it was replaced by the civil code from 1950. Despite fact that some parts were in effect until 1964, the civil code had never been translated and published in Czech. The new Czech civil code (2012) took its inspiration from the ABGB again. In southern Poland it was partially in effect until the end of 1946. In Bosnia and Herzegovina , it was partially introduced after 1878, and some parts are still in use, even after this country's independence. References [ edit ] ^ "ÖNB-ALEX - Justizgesetzsammlung" . alex.onb.ac.at . Retrieved 2024-03-13 . Further reading [ edit ] Eschig, Peter (2021). Das österreichische ABGB - The Austrian Civil Code (2nd ed.). LexisNexis ARD ORAC. ISBN 978-3-7007-7795-3 . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Austrian Civil Code § 1295, which establishes general liability for damages caused by fault, by limiting the DISCLOSEE's liability to gross negligence or willful misconduct. The original text held the DISCLOSEE liable for 'any and all losses,' which aligns with the broader standard of liability. The modified text narrows this to 'gross negligence or willful misconduct,' contradicting the standard under Austrian law, which includes ordinary negligence, as the Law Explanation states. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the 'Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch,' which after preparatory work was published on 1 June 1811 and came into force on 1 January 1812.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement is governed by Austrian law, to the exclusion of the UN Convention on the International Sale of Goods and the IPRG [law on in ternational private law]. Jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is generally guided by principles similar to Austrian law, as the parties understand them, to the exclusion of the UN Convention on the International Sale of Goods and the IPRG [law on in ternational private law]. Disputes will be addressed through amicable negotiation, ideally in Wels.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'is governed by Austrian law' with 'is generally guided by principles similar to Austrian law, as the parties understand them'. It also weakens the jurisdiction clause by changing 'Jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels' to 'Disputes will be addressed through amicable negotiation, ideally in Wels'. The interpretation of Austrian law as the parties 'understand them' removes objectivity, and attempting 'amicable negotiation' instead of a clear jurisdiction provides uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian Civil Code § 6 states that the laws cannot be altered by private agreements. Replacing the specific jurisdiction of Austrian law with a subjective 'understanding' of its principles directly violates this principle by attempting to bypass the objective application of the law. Similarly, stating that disputes will be addressed through negotiation first avoids established dispute resolution processes by the jurisdiction of Wels.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TALL KNIGHT We are the keepers of the sacred words. NI ... Peng ... and Neee ... Wom! BEDEVERE Those who hear them seldom live to tell the tale. TALL KNIGHT The Knights Who Say 'Ni'! demand a sacrifice. ARTHUR (to the TALL KNIGHT) Knights Who Say 'Ni' ... we are but simple travellers. We seek the Enchanter who lives beyond this wood and who ... TALL KNIGHT NI! ARTHUR (recoiling) Oh! TALL KNIGHT NI! NI! ARTHUR (he cowers in fear) Oh!
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly states that it 'is governed by Austrian law,' creating a definitive legal obligation. The changed text replaces this with 'generally guided by principles similar to Austrian law, as the parties understand them,' introducing ambiguity and subjectivity. The Law Explanation states that Austrian Civil Code § 6 dictates that laws cannot be altered by private agreements, and this modification directly violates that principle by allowing a subjective interpretation of the law. The original text establishes a clear jurisdiction at Wels, while the modified text suggests 'amicable negotiation, ideally in Wels,' weakening the jurisdictional clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant. However, the Law Explanation is clear and compelling. This change creates a significant ambiguity, making the modified contract non-compliant and potentially unenforceable under Austrian law, as the application of the law is now based on the parties' subjective understanding, rather than the objective legal standards.
